content.assessment: This proposal identifies a legally distinct and potentially more tractable lever: anti-discrimination exemption review, exemplified by the 2015 Curia ruling permitting segregated Church-maintained schools. Unlike track-age or admissions reform, this pathway engages judicial and civil-rights enforcement machinery directly, but it simultaneously invites direct legal confrontation with maintainer autonomy and prior jurisprudence protective of religious-institution discretion, and centralization under KLIK has curtailed the municipal planning autonomy needed for zoning-based levers.
opportunities: Legal review of anti-discrimination exemptions could establish new accountability standards applicable across maintainer types, including non-state schools otherwise outside ministerial reach.
strengths: Targets a specific, already-identified legal lever — anti-discrimination exemptions — offering a concrete enforceability pathway distinct from politically fraught admissions reform.
Operates without requiring amendment to the core statutory tracking provisions, reducing the legislative-authority threshold relative to structural reform.
threats: Legal challenge risk is explicit and precedent-based, given the Curia's prior ruling; any reform attempt likely triggers renewed litigation.
Prior desegregation measures' stagnation suggests that even a legally sound lever may face durability limits without sustained political commitment.
weaknesses: Curia precedent upholding a Church-maintainer segregation exemption creates an adverse legal baseline that any exemption-review effort must directly overturn or narrow.
Centralization under KLIK has reduced municipal planning autonomy, constraining the local governance capacity needed to implement zoning changes.
